planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches at least 70°F; Cowhorn Okra is an old Southern heirloom named for its unusually long (8–12 inch), slightly curved, pale green pods — much longer than the standard 3–4 inch pods of modern varieties like Clemson Spineless — and is prized by traditional Southern gardeners who prefer large pods for okra pickles and who enjoy the long harvesting window before pods become fibrous.

directions: Soak seeds 8–12 hours in warm water before planting to improve germination; direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun, well-drained soil, 12–18 inches apart; harvest pods at 6–8 inches for tender eating quality — Cowhorn pods remain tender longer than standard varieties at larger sizes. Cowhorn Okra is a traditional Southern heirloom variety whose long, pale green, slightly curved pods inspired the name — resembling the curved horn of a cow — and it has been grown throughout the Gulf South and Deep South for generations as the preferred okra of gardeners who want large pods for pickling and traditional okra dishes. The plants are vigorous and tall (4–6 feet), producing prolifically through the summer heat of zone 9 and the Gulf South, and the pods remain tender at longer lengths (6–8 inches) compared to modern commercial varieties that become fibrous past 3–4 inches. This extended tenderness window makes Cowhorn particularly valued for whole-pod pickling, where large uniform pods are cut to fit mason jars, and for large-format gumbo preparations where longer pod segments are preferred. Cowhorn is a classic open-pollinated heirloom that can be saved seed from year to year; the plants also reseed readily in warm climates if any pods are left to mature. An important ecological note: okra in general is adapted to the same climate as cotton (to which it is related in the Malvaceae family) and handles the heat, humidity, and occasional drought of Gulf Coast summers better than almost any other food crop.

common_name: Allsweet Watermelon (Striped Oblong Southern Heirloom Watermelon)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is 70°F or warmer; Allsweet is a large-fruited heirloom watermelon producing classic oblong fruits with dark green skin and bold pale green stripes weighing 25–35 pounds — one of the traditional 'picnic watermelons' prized for its high sugar content, bright red flesh, and excellent keeping quality after harvest; one of the defining watermelon varieties of Southern U.S. home gardens.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills of 3–4 seeds (thin to 2 plants) or individual transplants after soil warms; provide ample space (6–10 feet in all directions) for vigorous vines; stop irrigation when fruits are nearly ripe to concentrate sugars; test ripeness by thumping (dull thud), checking the ground spot (turning yellow), or pressing on the vine end of the fruit. Allsweet is a large, oblong, open-pollinated watermelon developed and marketed by Dessert Seed Company that became one of the most widely grown home garden and commercial watermelons in the Southern states during the latter decades of the 20th century. The classic appearance — elongated oval fruits averaging 25–35 pounds with dark green skin boldly marked by lighter green stripes, bright red flesh, and black seeds in the traditional style — made Allsweet the archetypical 'picnic watermelon' of the American South. The flavor lives up to the name: exceptionally sweet (Brix 12–14), with the characteristic deep red color and firm, crisp texture that distinguished traditional open-pollinated watermelons from modern seedless varieties. Allsweet produces fruits with good keeping quality after harvest — they hold well in cool storage for 2–3 weeks — and the vines are productive and disease-tolerant under Gulf Coast and Deep South summer conditions. A standard comparison point against which other traditional watermelons are measured in Southern gardens, and an important landmark in the history of American watermelon breeding.

common_name: Mississippi Silver Southern Pea (Silver-Hull Crowder Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil warms above 65°F; Mississippi Silver is a semi-erect bush type Southern pea producing distinctive silver-tan, slightly inflated pods with a speckled cream-and-purple seed — one of the most productive and flavorful crowder-type Southern peas grown in Mississippi and the Gulf South, and a staple of traditional Southern summer gardens for many decades.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun, well-drained soil; Mississippi Silver can be succession-planted every 3–4 weeks from spring through late summer for continuous production; plants fix atmospheric nitrogen and require no supplemental fertilizer in average garden soil; harvest at the green-shell stage when pods are well-filled and slightly silvery-tan in color. Mississippi Silver is a traditional open-pollinated Southern pea variety that derives its name from the distinctive silvery-tan to pale buff color of its mature pods — a different visual marker from the purple-hull and black-eyed types more familiar to outside the South. The variety is classified as a crowder type because the seeds are tightly packed ('crowded') inside the pod, causing them to develop flat sides from pressing against each other — the classic crowder characteristic that gives the type its name. The seeds are cream-colored with purple or red-purple speckled markings that fade to a uniform tan when cooked, as with most field peas, and the flavor is the rich, earthy, savory character of Southern field peas that is quite different from garden peas or snap beans. Mississippi Silver is one of the most widely recommended varieties by Mississippi State University Extension and LSU AgCenter for home garden Southern pea production — consistently rated highly for both yield and eating quality in regional trials. Like all Southern peas (Vigna unguiculata), it thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf South through summer months when most other vegetables have ended, making it one of the primary summer food crops of the region.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer to early fall for fall/winter/spring production in zones 7–11; Senposai is a Japanese hybrid leafy green that is significantly more heat-tolerant than standard cabbages and brassicas, making it one of the better cool-season leafy greens for the Gulf South where the heat ends conventional brassica production early — it continues producing into warmer temperatures than kale, collards, or Chinese cabbage.

directions: Direct sow 1/4 inch deep and thin to 8–14 inches apart; harvest outer leaves continuously as cut-and-come-again from about 30 days; Senposai is mild enough to eat raw as well as cooked; spring plantings should go in very early since it bolts in sustained heat. Senposai is a hybrid leafy green developed in Japan by crossing common cabbage (Brassica oleracea) with komatsuna or Japanese mustard spinach (Brassica rapa var. perviridis), producing a vigorous, fast-growing green that combines characteristics of both parents — the large, smooth, broadly oval leaves of cabbage with the faster maturity, milder flavor, and somewhat greater heat tolerance of komatsuna. The resulting hybrid fills a useful niche in both Japanese home gardens and warm-climate gardens worldwide: it is ready for harvest significantly faster than heading cabbages (40–60 days versus 80–120), produces continuously as a cut-and-come-again crop rather than requiring a single heading harvest, and tolerates heat better than most brassicas while retaining the mild, slightly sweet, cabbage-family flavor without the sharpness of mustard greens. Senposai is versatile in the kitchen — the large, smooth leaves are excellent raw in salads, quickly stir-fried or sautéed, braised in Japanese-style dashimono or miso soup, or used like savoy cabbage leaves for wrapping. In zone 9a and the Gulf South, Senposai is most valuable as a fall-through-spring cool-season green, sown from September through February for harvest from October through April. Available from Kitazawa Seed Company and other Asian vegetable seed specialists.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture and Human Resources

common_name: Louisiana Green Velvet Okra (Long Smooth Dark Green Southern Okra)

planting.note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; Louisiana Green Velvet is a smooth-podded, heavy-yielding heirloom okra variety that produces long (5–7 inch), straight, smooth dark green pods with minimal ribbing and particularly tender texture — one of the top-recommended varieties by LSU AgCenter for Gulf Coast okra production, prized for consistent tenderness and yield through humid Louisiana summers.

directions: Soak seeds overnight before planting; direct sow 1 inch deep 12–18 inches apart after soil warms; harvest pods at 4–5 inches for the finest texture; once production begins, harvest every 2 days during peak summer heat as pods elongate rapidly. Louisiana Green Velvet is an heirloom open-pollinated okra variety with a strong association with Louisiana and Gulf Coast traditional food culture — it is one of the most historically prominent varieties in the region and has been grown by Louisiana gardeners for generations as the standard for gumbo okra and fresh summer side dishes. The pods are smooth, deep green, and slightly ridged rather than heavily ribbed like Clemson Spineless, with a velvety (not bristly) surface texture that makes handling comfortable and gives the variety its name. 'Green Velvet' pods remain tender at 4–5 inches under high summer temperatures — a practical advantage in the Gulf South where summer heat causes many okra varieties to become tough and fibrous quickly if not harvested every day. The variety is adapted to the humid, hot climate of coastal Louisiana and the Gulf South and performs especially well in the clay soils and high humidity of the Louisiana bayou region where it originated as a preferred variety. Louisiana Green Velvet is the traditional okra of Cajun and Creole cooking — most commonly used in gumbo, where the natural mucilage (thickening quality) of properly cooked okra is considered essential to authentic texture and flavor.
